title: UN Security Council Convenes Emergency Session Over Gaza Ceasefire Collapse
summary: World leaders demand accountability as fragile truce breaks down along the Gaza corridor, leaving humanitarian aid convoys stranded and millions at risk.
content:
The United Nations Security Council convened an emergency session on Monday after a fragile ceasefire in Gaza broke down overnight, with both Israeli forces and Hamas-aligned factions trading accusations of initiating the renewed hostilities.

Ambassadors from France, the United Kingdom, and the United States delivered urgent statements calling for an immediate return to negotiations, while Russia and China jointly demanded an independent international inquiry into the collapse of the truce brokered just eleven days earlier under Egyptian mediation.

"The international community cannot afford another cycle of escalation," UN Secretary-General António Guterres said in opening remarks. "Every hour of delay costs lives that cannot be recovered."

Aid organizations operating in the territory reported that at least fourteen humanitarian convoys have been stranded since the resumption of hostilities, with access routes around the Rafah and Kerem Shalom crossings sealed by military operations. The World Food Programme estimates that over 2.3 million people in Gaza remain critically dependent on external food supplies.

The session concluded without a binding resolution after the United States exercised its veto power over a draft text that would have demanded an immediate and permanent ceasefire, calling the language "unenforceable" without greater accountability mechanisms.

title: G20 Summit in Johannesburg Closes With Landmark Climate Finance Pact
summary: Developed nations pledge $500 billion over a decade to support green transitions in emerging economies, though critics call the timeline dangerously slow.
content:
The G20 summit in Johannesburg concluded on Sunday with the signing of a landmark climate finance agreement under which wealthy member states committed to mobilizing $500 billion over ten years to support clean energy transitions in developing and emerging economies.

The deal was brokered over three days of intensive negotiations and was hailed by South African President Cyril Ramaphosa as a turning point in the global response to the climate crisis.

However, environmental groups expressed frustration that the bulk of the financing would come in the form of loans rather than grants, arguing that debt-laden nations could not afford to borrow their way to a green economy.

The United States and European Union framed the agreement as a pragmatic compromise that could be implemented immediately, while China announced a separate bilateral initiative to invest in renewable energy infrastructure across fifteen African nations.

title: India-Pakistan Diplomatic Channel Reopens After Three-Year Freeze
summary: Back-channel talks facilitated by Oman have yielded a provisional agreement to resume consular services and ease trade restrictions along the Wagah border.
content:
India and Pakistan have agreed to reopen diplomatic channels frozen since the 2023 Pahalgam crisis, following months of back-channel talks facilitated by Oman that have yielded a provisional framework for resuming consular services and easing certain trade restrictions at the Wagah-Attari border crossing.

The agreement, confirmed by foreign ministry spokespersons in both New Delhi and Islamabad on Thursday, falls well short of a full normalization of relations but represents the most significant diplomatic development on the subcontinent in years.

Under the terms of the provisional arrangement, both countries will reinstate visa processing for a limited set of humanitarian categories including medical travel, family reunification, and religious pilgrimages within 60 days.

Regional analysts credited Oman's Foreign Minister with engineering the talks, leveraging Muscat's longstanding relationships with both capitals and its reputation for discreet mediation.
